methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $5M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 12 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: Foundation Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), post-money $3B+ (reported, raise in progress)

data.narrative:
Corvex Systems is a Seed-stage robotics and manufacturing company that has raised $5M+. The valuation landscape includes one public comparable (277810.KQ) and eight private peers in the Robotics & Manufacturing sector, including 1X Technologies, Foundation Robotics, Divergent, Exotec, and Locus Robotics. However, the peer set lacks sufficient same-stage or narrowly comparable datapoints to support a robust peer-median approach—only one adjacent-stage peer was identified within relevant filters, below the three-comparable threshold required for reliable triangulation.

The Stage-Progression method is the primary applicable approach. Using the $5M total capital raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.5x multiplier typical for Seed-to-next-stage progression yields an implied valuation of $12M. This method assumes the company has achieved sufficient technical or commercial milestones to command that step-up multiplier, standard for robotic systems companies graduating from initial seed validation.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $11M to $14M, centered on a $12M median. This range reflects the single-method triangulation and inherent uncertainty from sparse comparable data at the Seed stage. Key risks to this range include: (1) the typical step-up multiplier may not apply if Corvex lacks the expected technical differentiation or customer traction; (2) broader peer comparables in robotics are skewed toward more mature private and public firms, limiting precision; and (3) frontier-tech Seed valuations are highly dependent on team quality and IP defensibility, neither quantified here.
